headline: Body-Snatching Disputes: 28 Cases in 5 Years Over Burial Rights
context: MCCBCHST recorded 28 cases between 2020 and 2025 where deceased persons' bodies were claimed by Islamic religious authorities against family wishes, citing alleged conversion.

cards.big: 28 families fought for the right to bury their own relatives.
cards.sub: In each case, state religious authorities claimed the deceased had converted to Islam — often without documentary proof.

cards.big: The burden of proving conversion falls on the family contesting it.
cards.sub: Religious authorities present a registration entry. Families must prove it false — an evidentiary asymmetry.

cards.big: 12 of 28 cases involved Orang Asli or indigenous individuals.
cards.sub: Conversion registration processes in rural areas have been documented as lacking informed consent.

cards.big: Average court resolution time for body-snatching cases is 14 months.
cards.sub: Islamic burial requires same-day interment. By the time courts decide, the body has been buried.

cards.big: When burial is same-day but court resolution takes 14 months, the law cannot protect the rights it is supposed to guarantee.
An interim preservation order system — holding the body pending adjudication — would respect both Islamic burial norms and family rights. The current system's speed makes justice impossible.
